Experiment 3: Decision Tree Classifier (Implementing the ID3 or CART algorithm, visualizing the tree structure, and analyzing the impact of pruning)
Experiment 4: Support Vector Machines (SVM) (Applying SVM for binary and multi-class classification using different kernels like Linear, Polynomial, and RBF)
Experiment 5: Naive Bayes Classifier (Building a probabilistic classifier for text classification or medical diagnosis based on Bayes' Theorem)
Experiment 6: K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) (Implementing the proximity-based classifier and finding the optimal 'K' value using the Elbow method)
Experiment 7: K-Means Clustering (Implementing unsupervised grouping of data and visualizing clusters and centroids)
Experiment 8: Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) (Designing a basic Neural Network with backpropagation for pattern recognition)
Experiment 9: Ensemble Learning (Implementing Random Forest or Boosting techniques to improve prediction accuracy)
Experiment 10: Model Evaluation Metrics (Generating Confusion Matrices, calculating Precision, Recall, and F1-Score, and plotting ROC curves)
course_outcomes: CO1: Clean and transform raw datasets into a format suitable for machine learning models.
CO2: Implement and tune supervised learning algorithms for classification and regression tasks.
CO3: Apply unsupervised learning techniques to discover natural groupings in data.
CO4: Build and train artificial neural networks for complex pattern recognition.
CO5: Quantitatively evaluate model performance using statistical metrics and visualization tools.

name: Blockchain Technology
description: Explores the fundamental concepts of distributed ledger technology. Covers cryptographic hashing, digital signatures, consensus algorithms (PoW, PoS), smart contracts, and the architecture of platforms like Bitcoin and Ethereum.
topics: Module-1: Introduction to Blockchain (Distributed Ledger Technology (DLT), Centralized vs. Decentralized systems, History of Bitcoin, Blockchain structure: Blocks, Chaining, and Merkle Trees)
Module-2: Cryptographic Foundations (Cryptographic Hash Functions: SHA-256, Properties of hashes, Digital Signatures, Public Key Infrastructure (PKI), Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC))
Module-3: Consensus Mechanisms (The Byzantine Generals Problem, Proof of Work (PoW), Proof of Stake (PoS), Delegated PoS, Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT), Mining and Incentives)

Module-4: Smart Contracts and Ethereum (Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM), Introduction to Solidity programming, Gas fees, Decentralized Applications (DApps), Decentralized Autonomous Organizations (DAO))
Module-5: Applications and Future Trends (Blockchain in Supply Chain, Healthcare, and Finance; Hyperledger Fabric for Enterprise; Scaling solutions: Sharding and Layer-2; Web3 and Metaverse)
course_outcomes: CO1: Explain the structural components and decentralized nature of blockchain technology.
CO2: Apply cryptographic principles to ensure data integrity and security within a ledger.
CO3: Analyze and compare different consensus algorithms and their impact on network security and scalability.
CO4: Design and develop basic smart contracts using Solidity for decentralized applications.
CO5: Identify and evaluate real-world use cases for blockchain across various industrial sectors.

name: Computer Vision
description: Explores advanced techniques for extracting meaningful information from digital images and videos. Focuses on the integration of Deep Learning (CNNs), object detection frameworks (YOLO, R-CNN), and real-world applications in medical imaging and autonomous systems.
topics: Module-1: Review of Image Processing and Foundations (Digital image fundamentals, Spatial and Frequency domain filtering, Image restoration, Color image processing)
Module-2: Feature Extraction and Image Descriptors (Texture analysis, Shape descriptors, Local features: SIFT, SURF, and BRIEF; Principal Component Analysis (PCA) for vision)
Module-3: Deep Learning for Vision (Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) architecture: Convolution, Pooling, and Fully Connected layers; Popular architectures: AlexNet, VGG, ResNet)
Module-4: Object Detection and Recognition (Region-based CNNs (R-CNN, Fast R-CNN, Faster R-CNN), YOLO (You Only Look Once) framework, SSD (Single Shot MultiBox Detector), Face recognition techniques)
Module-5: Advanced Segmentation and Applications (Semantic vs. Instance segmentation, U-Net architecture, Image captioning, Vision in autonomous vehicles, Medical image analysis)
course_outcomes: CO1: Apply advanced image restoration and enhancement techniques in the frequency domain.
CO2: Evaluate and implement robust feature descriptors for image matching and retrieval.
CO3: Design and train Convolutional Neural Networks for image classification tasks.
CO4: Implement state-of-the-art object detection frameworks like YOLO for real-time applications.
CO5: Develop specialized vision systems for domains such as healthcare and robotics.

name: Internet of Things
description: Explores the end-to-end architecture of IoT systems. Covers sensor technology, communication protocols (MQTT, CoAP), microcontrollers (Arduino, Raspberry Pi), cloud integration, and the security challenges of connecting physical devices to the internet.
topics: Module-1: Introduction to IoT (Definition, Characteristics, Physical and Logical design, IoT enabling technologies, IoT levels and deployment templates)
Module-2: IoT Hardware and Platforms (Introduction to Arduino and Raspberry Pi, Sensor types: Temperature, Humidity, Motion, Gas; Actuators: Motors, Relays; Programming GPIO pins)
Module-3: IoT Networking and Protocols (Link Layer: 802.15.4, ZigBee, LoRaWAN; Network Layer: IPv6, 6LoWPAN; Application Layer Protocols: MQTT, CoAP, XMPP, AMQP)
Module-4: IoT Data Analytics and Cloud (Cloud computing for IoT, Data collection and storage, Real-time data processing, IoT to Cloud communication, Using platforms like AWS IoT or ThingsBoard)
Module-5: Security, Ethics, and Applications (IoT Security challenges, Privacy issues, Case studies: Smart Home, Smart Cities, Agriculture 4.0 (Smart Farming), Industrial IoT (IIoT))
course_outcomes: CO1: Design the basic architectural framework for a distributed IoT system.
CO2: Interface various sensors and actuators with microcontrollers like Raspberry Pi or Arduino.
CO3: Select and implement appropriate communication protocols based on power and bandwidth constraints.
CO4: Integrate IoT edge devices with cloud platforms for data storage and analysis.
CO5: Develop secure and ethical IoT solutions for real-world domains such as agriculture and healthcare.

name: Generative AI Laboratory
description: Practical hands-on course covering the lifecycle of Generative AI applications. Includes working with word embeddings, prompt engineering, Hugging Face transformers, and building functional AI agents like chatbots using frameworks such as LangChain.
topics: Experiment 1 & 2: Word Embeddings (Exploring pre-trained vectors, vector arithmetic, and using PCA/t-SNE to visualize semantic clusters in a 2D space)
Experiment 3: Domain-Specific Training (Training custom Word2Vec models on specialized datasets like medical or legal corpora to capture unique semantics)
Experiment 4 & 5: Prompt Engineering (Using embeddings to retrieve similar words and 'enrich' prompts; evaluating how prompt context affects model output quality)
Experiment 6 & 7: Transformer Pipelines (Implementing Hugging Face pipelines for Sentiment Analysis and Text Summarization using pre-trained models)
Experiment 8 & 9: LangChain & API Integration (Setting up LangChain with Cohere/OpenAI APIs, creating prompt templates, and parsing structured data from web sources)
Experiment 10: RAG-based Chatbot (Building a Retrieval-Augmented Generation chatbot, such as a legal advisor for the IPC, using document loaders and vector stores)
course_outcomes: CO1: Manipulate and visualize high-dimensional word embeddings to understand semantic relationships.
CO2: Design and optimize prompts using techniques like 'Few-Shot' and 'Enrichment' to guide Generative AI outputs.
CO3: Deploy pre-trained transformer models for various NLP tasks via the Hugging Face ecosystem.
CO4: Integrate LLMs into application workflows using LangChain for automated data retrieval and parsing.
CO5: Develop and evaluate a functional RAG-based chatbot capable of context-aware conversations.

name: DevOps Laboratory
description: Practical course on the tools and practices used to automate software delivery. Covers version control, continuous integration/continuous deployment (CI/CD) pipelines, containerization, and infrastructure as code.
topics: Experiment 1: Advanced Version Control (Branching strategies in Git, resolving merge conflicts, and using Git Hooks for pre-commit checks)
Experiment 2: Containerization with Docker (Creating Dockerfiles, managing images, and using Docker Compose to orchestrate multi-container applications)
Experiment 3: Continuous Integration (CI) (Setting up Jenkins or GitHub Actions to automatically build and test code upon every push)
Experiment 4: Continuous Deployment (CD) (Automating the deployment of containerized applications to cloud platforms or local servers)
Experiment 5: Build Tools (Configuring Maven or Gradle for Java projects and handling dependency management)
Experiment 6: Infrastructure as Code (IaC) (Introduction to Terraform or Ansible for automating server setup and configuration)
Experiment 7: Static Code Analysis (Integrating tools like SonarQube into the pipeline to check for code quality and security vulnerabilities)
Experiment 8: Monitoring and Logging (Setting up basic monitoring using tools like Prometheus or Grafana to track application health)
course_outcomes: CO1: Master advanced Git workflows to manage collaborative software development.
CO2: Package applications and their dependencies into portable Docker containers.
CO3: Design and implement automated CI/CD pipelines to reduce manual intervention.
CO4: Automate infrastructure provisioning using Infrastructure as Code (IaC) principles.
CO5: Monitor application performance and ensure high quality through automated static analysis.

name: Project Management
description: Covers the complete project lifecycle from inception to termination. Focuses on project selection strategies, Work Breakdown Structures (WBS), resource leveling, risk management, and quantitative scheduling techniques like PERT and CPM.
topics: Module-1: Introduction and Project Selection (Definition and characteristics of projects; Project Life Cycle phases; Strategic planning process; Project selection methods: Financial models (NPV, IRR) and Scoring models)
Module-2: Project Planning and Scheduling (Defining project scope; Work Breakdown Structure (WBS); Scheduling basics; Gantt Charts; Network construction rules; Monte Carlo simulation basics)
Module-3: Resourcing and Risk Management (Abilities needed for resourcing; Cost planning, estimating, and budgeting; Risk identification and analysis; Project quality tools; Project kick-off)

Module-4: Execution and Progress (Project supply chain management; Contract types; Partnering and collaboration; Knowledge Management; Project Balanced Scorecard; Finishing and terminating the project)
Module-5: Network Analysis (Critical Path Method (CPM): Floats and slack; Program Evaluation and Review Technique (PERT): Expected duration, variance, and probability of completion; Project Crashing)
course_outcomes: CO1: Explain the project lifecycle and apply strategic models to select high-value projects.
CO2: Develop a comprehensive Work Breakdown Structure and initial project schedules.
CO3: Plan resources, estimate costs, and develop risk mitigation strategies.
CO4: Manage the project execution phase using supply chain and quality tools.
CO5: Perform quantitative network analysis using PERT/CPM to predict and optimize project completion times.

name: Internet of Things
description: Comprehensive study of IoT architecture, design methodologies, and physical devices. Covers the integration of sensors, microcontrollers (Arduino/Raspberry Pi), communication protocols, and data analytics using big data frameworks.
topics: Module-1: Introduction to IoT (Physical and Logical Design of IoT; IoT Enabling Technologies; IoT Levels & Deployment Templates: Level-1 to Level-6 architectures)
Module-2: IoT and M2M (Introduction to Machine-to-Machine (M2M) communication; SDN and NFV for IoT; IoT System Management with NETCONF-YANG and SNMP)
Module-3: IoT Platforms Design Methodology (IoT Design Methodology steps; Case Study on Weather Monitoring; Logical Design using Python: Data types, Control flow, and Classes)

Module-4: IoT Physical Devices & Endpoints (What is an IoT Device; Raspberry Pi board details; Linux on Raspberry Pi; Interfaces and Programming with Python; Case Studies: Home Automation, Smart Cities, Agriculture)
Module-5: Data Analytics for IoT (Introduction to Big Data; Apache Hadoop: MapReduce, YARN, HDFS; Apache Spark and Storm for real-time data analysis)
course_outcomes: CO1: Describe the evolution of IoT, its networking components, and addressing strategies.
CO2: Analyze various sensing devices, actuator types, and communication models.
CO3: Design IoT systems using a structured methodology and Python-based logical design.
CO4: Implement IoT solutions by programming physical devices like Raspberry Pi.
CO5: Utilize big data frameworks like Hadoop and Spark to perform analytics on IoT-generated data.

name: Big Data Analytics
description: Explores the architectures and tools required to process and analyze massive datasets. Covers the Hadoop ecosystem (HDFS, MapReduce, Hive, Pig), NoSQL databases like MongoDB, and large-scale data processing with Apache Spark, integrated with machine learning applications.
topics: Module-1: Introduction to Big Data (Characteristics and Evolution of Big Data; Traditional BI vs. Big Data; Hadoop environment; Introduction to MapReduce: Mapper, Reducer, Combiner, Partitioner)
Module-2: Hadoop Ecosystem & NoSQL (HDFS architecture and commands; Introduction to NoSQL; MongoDB: Architecture, Data types, and CRUD operations; High availability and Sharding)
Module-3: Advanced MapReduce & Programming (MapReduce workflows; Matrix Multiplication and Weather Data mining programs; Debugging MapReduce; Introduction to Apache Pig and Pig Latin)
Module-4: Data Warehousing with Hive (Hive Architecture and Data types; Hive Query Language (HQL); RC File implementation; User Defined Functions (UDFs); Comparison of Pig vs. Hive)
Module-5: Real-time Analytics with Spark (Introduction to Apache Spark; Data analysis with Spark RDDs; Text, Web Content, and Link Analytics; Machine Learning applications in Big Data contexts)
course_outcomes: CO1: Explain the concepts of Big Data and the fundamental architecture of the Hadoop framework.
CO2: Design and implement MapReduce programs for complex data processing tasks like matrix multiplication.
CO3: Utilize NoSQL databases like MongoDB for storing and managing unstructured data at scale.
CO4: Develop data flow scripts in Pig and perform data warehousing operations using HiveQL.
CO5: Apply Apache Spark for real-time data analysis and implement large-scale machine learning solutions.

name: Parallel Computing
description: Introduction to the architectures and programming models for parallel systems. Covers parallel algorithm design, performance analysis (Amdahl's Law), and hands-on programming with OpenMP for shared memory and MPI for distributed memory systems.
topics: Module-1: Introduction and Architectures (Motivations for parallel computing; Flynn’s Taxonomy: SISD, SIMD, MISD, MIMD; Hardware architectures: Multi-core, GPU, Cluster computing)
Module-2: Parallel Algorithm Design (Foster’s Design Methodology: Partitioning, Communication, Agglomeration, and Mapping; Decomposition techniques: Task vs. Data decomposition)
Module-3: Performance Analysis (Speedup, Efficiency, and Scalability; Amdahl’s Law vs. Gustafson’s Law; Overheads in parallel systems: Contention, Latency, and Synchronization)

Module-4: Shared Memory Programming with OpenMP (Thread-based parallelism; Directives: parallel, for, sections; Data environment: Shared vs. Private variables; Synchronization: Critical, Atomic, Barrier)

Module-5: Distributed Memory Programming with MPI (Process-based parallelism; Point-to-point communication: send, recv; Collective communication: Broadcast, Scatter, Gather, Reduce; Writing and debugging MPI programs)
course_outcomes: CO1: Classify parallel architectures based on memory organization and instruction streams.
CO2: Apply formal design methodologies to transform serial algorithms into parallel versions.
CO3: Quantify the performance gains and limitations of parallel systems using analytical laws.
CO4: Develop and optimize multi-threaded applications using the OpenMP API.
CO5: Create scalable distributed applications using the Message Passing Interface (MPI) standard.

name: Cryptography and Network Security
description: Explores the principles and practice of cryptography and network security. Covers classical and modern encryption techniques, public-key infrastructure, hash functions, digital signatures, and security protocols for email, IP, and the web.
topics: Module-1: Introduction and Classical Encryption (Security Goals: Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability; Security Attacks, Services, and Mechanisms; Classical techniques: Caesar, Playfair, Hill Cipher, Transposition techniques)

Module-2: Symmetric Key Cryptography (Block Ciphers and Data Encryption Standard (DES); Advanced Encryption Standard (AES) structure and transformation functions; Block cipher modes of operation: ECB, CBC, CFB, OFB, CTR)

Module-3: Public Key Cryptography and Hash Functions (Number Theory basics: Prime numbers, Fermat’s and Euler’s Theorems; RSA Algorithm; Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange; Cryptographic Hash Functions: SHA-512; Message Authentication Codes (MAC))
Module-4: Digital Signatures and Key Management (Digital Signature Standard (DSS); User Authentication: Kerberos, X.509 Certificates; Public Key Infrastructure (PKI); Trust models)

Module-5: Network and Internet Security (Transport Level Security: SSL/TLS; Email Security: PGP and S/MIME; IP Security (IPsec) architecture; Firewalls: Packet filtering, Stateful inspection, Application gateways; Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS))
course_outcomes: CO1: Classify different security attacks and apply classical encryption techniques to secure data.
CO2: Implement and analyze modern symmetric key algorithms like AES for high-speed data encryption.
CO3: Utilize public-key cryptography and RSA to solve key distribution and non-repudiation problems.
CO4: Generate and verify digital signatures and message authentication codes to ensure data integrity.
CO5: Design secure network architectures using firewalls, IDS, and encrypted transport protocols like SSL/TLS.

name: Deep Learning
description: An advanced course on deep neural networks. Covers the mathematical foundations of backpropagation, specialized architectures like CNNs for vision, RNNs/LSTMs for sequences, and modern generative models including GANs and Autoencoders.
topics: Module-1: Introduction to Deep Learning (Biological vs. Artificial Neurons; Perceptrons and Multi-layer Perceptrons; Activation Functions: Sigmoid, Tanh, ReLU; Loss Functions and Optimization: Gradient Descent, Adam, RMSProp)
Module-2: Training Deep Networks (The Backpropagation algorithm; Vanishing and Exploding Gradient problems; Regularization techniques: L1/L2, Dropout, Batch Normalization; Hyperparameter tuning)

Module-3: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) (Convolution operation and Filters; Pooling layers; Architecture of famous CNNs: LeNet, VGG, ResNet; Applications in Object Recognition and Image Segmentation)

Module-4: Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) (Sequential data modeling; Unfolding computational graphs; Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks; Gated Recurrent Units (GRU); Applications in Language Modeling and Time-series prediction)

Module-5: Generative and Advanced Models (Autoencoders and Variational Autoencoders (VAEs); Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs): Generator and Discriminator dynamics; Introduction to Attention mechanisms and Transformers)
course_outcomes: CO1: Analyze the fundamental components and mathematical foundations of deep neural networks.
CO2: Implement and optimize backpropagation to train multi-layer neural architectures.
CO3: Design and deploy Convolutional Neural Networks for complex visual recognition tasks.
CO4: Utilize Recurrent Neural Networks to process and predict sequential or temporal data.
CO5: Explore and evaluate generative models and advanced architectures like GANs for creative AI applications.

name: Natural Language Processing
description: Introduction to the theory and practice of NLP. Covers text preprocessing, language modeling, syntactic and semantic analysis, and advanced topics like sequence-to-sequence models and the Transformer architecture.
topics: Module-1: Introduction and Text Processing (History and challenges of NLP; Regular expressions; Tokenization, Lemmatization, and Stemming; Word counts and Zipf’s Law; Edit Distance algorithms)
Module-2: Language Modeling and POS Tagging (N-grams and smoothing; Part-of-Speech (POS) tagging: Hidden Markov Models (HMM) and Viterbi algorithm; Rule-based vs. Stochastic tagging)
Module-3: Vector Semantics and Embeddings (Lexical semantics; TF-IDF; Word2Vec (Skip-gram and CBOW); Global Vectors (GloVe); Measuring semantic similarity with Cosine Similarity)
Module-4: Syntactic and Semantic Analysis (Context-Free Grammars (CFG); Dependency Parsing vs. Constituency Parsing; Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD); Named Entity Recognition (NER))
Module-5: Sequence Models and Transformers (Encoder-Decoder architectures; Attention mechanism; The Transformer model; Transfer Learning with BERT, GPT, and T5; Ethical considerations in NLP)
course_outcomes: CO1: Apply fundamental text preprocessing techniques to clean and normalize raw text data.
CO2: Build and evaluate N-gram language models and POS taggers for linguistic analysis.
CO3: Utilize vector space models and word embeddings to represent semantic relationships between words.
CO4: Implement parsing algorithms to analyze the grammatical structure and meaning of sentences.
CO5: Design and deploy modern Transformer-based models for complex tasks like translation and summarization.

name: Software Quality Assurance
description: Explores the methodologies and tools used to ensure software quality throughout the development lifecycle. Covers quality models, testing strategies (Manual and Automated), process improvement frameworks, and the distinction between Quality Assurance and Quality Control.
topics: Module-1: Fundamentals of Software Quality (Definition of Quality; SQA vs. Software Engineering; Quality Cost: Prevention, Appraisal, and Failure costs; SQA components and project lifecycle)
Module-2: SQA Components in Project Life Cycle (Contract review; Development and Quality plans; Review architectural components: Peer reviews, Walkthroughs, and Inspections)
Module-3: Software Testing Strategies (Black Box vs. White Box testing; Unit, Integration, System, and Acceptance testing; Regression testing; Automated testing tools and frameworks)
Module-4: Quality Management and Metrics (McCall’s Quality Model; Quality metrics: Product, Process, and Project metrics; Software configuration management; Documentation control)

Module-5: SQA Standards and Process Improvement (ISO 9001 and ISO 9000-3 standards; CMMI (Capability Maturity Model Integration) levels; Six Sigma in software quality; SQA for Agile and DevOps environments)
course_outcomes: CO1: Define software quality and identify the various costs associated with maintaining it.
CO2: Conduct effective peer reviews and inspections to identify defects early in the lifecycle.
CO3: Design and execute comprehensive test plans using both manual and automated strategies.
CO4: Apply quality models and metrics to quantitatively assess software products and processes.
CO5: Implement international quality standards and process improvement frameworks like CMMI and Six Sigma.

name: Social Network Analysis
description: Explores the structure and dynamics of social networks using graph theory and statistical models. Covers centrality measures, community detection, small-world phenomena, and the modeling of influence and information diffusion across large-scale networks.
topics: Module-1: Introduction to Social Networks (Graphs and Networks; Types of networks: Weighted, Directed, and Multimodal; Adjacency Matrix and List representations; Network metrics: Density, Degree, and Sparsity)
Module-2: Centrality and Power (Who is important?; Degree Centrality; Closeness Centrality; Betweenness Centrality; Eigenvector Centrality and the PageRank algorithm used by Google)

Module-3: Network Structure and Dynamics (Small-world phenomenon and 'Six Degrees of Separation'; Random networks vs. Scale-free networks; The Power Law distribution and 'The Rich get Richer' effect; Hubs and Authorities)

Module-4: Community Detection and Clustering (Identifying groups and sub-structures; Triads and Transitivity; Clustering Coefficients; Modularity and Community discovery algorithms like Louvain or Girvan-Newman)
Module-5: Influence and Diffusion (Information cascades; Epidemic modeling on networks: SI, SIR, and SIS models; Opinion dynamics; Link prediction and Recommender systems in social platforms)
course_outcomes: CO1: Represent real-world social systems as mathematical graphs and compute basic network metrics.
CO2: Identify influential actors and 'power players' within a network using various centrality measures.
CO3: Analyze the structural properties of large-scale networks, including small-world and scale-free characteristics.
CO4: Implement community detection algorithms to uncover hidden sub-groups and clusters within a network.
CO5: Model and simulate the spread of information, opinions, or viruses through a network structure.

name: Embedded Systems
description: An in-depth study of the design, analysis, and implementation of embedded computing systems. Covers hardware-software co-design, ARM processor architecture, Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS), memory management, and power-efficient system design.
topics: Module-1: Introduction to Embedded Systems (Definition and classification; The embedded design lifecycle; Characteristics and quality attributes; Challenges in embedded system design)
Module-2: Embedded Hardware and Processors (ARM Cortex-M architecture; Instruction sets and registers; I/O interfacing: GPIO, I2C, SPI, UART; Memory-mapped I/O; Memory architectures: SRAM vs. DRAM)

Module-3: Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS) (Concepts of RTOS: Tasks, Process, and Threads; Task scheduling: Round Robin vs. Priority-based; Inter-process communication: Semaphores, Mutexes, and Queues; Context switching)

Module-4: Hardware-Software Co-Design (Fundamental issues in hardware-software co-design; Computational models in embedded design: Data Flow Graphs (DFG) and State Machine models; Design space exploration)

Module-5: Embedded System Development and Security (Embedded C/C++ programming; Debugging techniques: JTAG, In-Circuit Emulators; Power management: Sleep modes and DVFS; Security in embedded systems: Root of Trust and Secure Boot)
course_outcomes: CO1: Analyze the specific constraints (power, memory, time) that define an embedded system.
CO2: Interface various peripherals and sensors with ARM-based microcontrollers using standard communication protocols.
CO3: Design and implement multi-tasking applications using a Real-Time Operating System (RTOS).
CO4: Utilize computational models to optimize the hardware-software trade-offs in a system design.
CO5: Develop, debug, and secure embedded firmware for real-world industrial and consumer applications.

name: Introduction to Non-Traditional Machining
description: An overview of advanced manufacturing processes that use unconventional energy sources. Covers Mechanical, Electrical, Chemical, and Thermal machining methods, highlighting their mechanisms, equipment, and industrial applications.
topics: Module-1: Introduction to NTM (Limitations of conventional machining; Classification of NTM processes based on energy source: Mechanical, Chemical, Electrochemical, and Thermal)
Module-2: Mechanical Process NTM (Ultrasonic Machining (USM): Mechanism, equipment, and variables; Abrasive Jet Machining (AJM): Process parameters and nozzle design; Water Jet Machining (WJM))

Module-3: Electrochemical and Chemical Machining (Electrochemical Machining (ECM): Study of electrolysis, process parameters, and tooling; Chemical Machining (CHM): Etchants and maskants used in chemical milling)
Module-4: Thermal Energy NTM - Part 1 (Electric Discharge Machining (EDM): Mechanism of metal removal, dielectric fluids, and spark generation; Wire-EDM (WEDM): Working principle and applications)
Module-5: Thermal Energy NTM - Part 2 (Laser Beam Machining (LBM): Types of lasers and thermal mechanisms; Plasma Arc Machining (PAM); Electron Beam Machining (EBM): Working in a vacuum environment)
course_outcomes: CO1: Compare conventional and non-traditional machining processes based on material properties and precision requirements.
CO2: Explain the material removal mechanisms in mechanical NTM processes like USM and AJM.
CO3: Analyze the parameters of Electrochemical and Chemical machining for high-precision components.
CO4: Evaluate the performance and applications of spark-based machining (EDM) in tool and die making.
CO5: Describe the working principles and safety considerations for high-energy beam machining (Laser, Electron, and Plasma).
